actions.thought: I am dealt 2c 5s on the button. This is an extremely weak, uncoordinated hand with almost no equity against any reasonable range. In a 3-handed game, even with position, playing this hand is significantly negative expected value. As this is the first hand of the tournament, discipline is paramount, and folding is the correct professional decision to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Ad Tc. After the Button folds, it is just me and the Big Blind. Ad Tc is a strong hand in a three-handed game. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and exert pressure on the Big Blind, as this hand is well ahead of the Big Blind's random range.

Defending KTo in the Big Blind against a Small Blind open raise is a standard play. The Small Blind often has a wide range in this spot when attempting to steal. 3-betting could overplay my hand and bloat the pot unnecessarily with a marginal holding, while folding is too tight. By calling, I keep his weaker hands in his range and can use my positional advantage post-flop.

On this dry Jack-high board, I maintain the range advantage as the pre-flop raiser. A continuation bet is standard here to put pressure on the Big Blind's range, which likely contains many missed hands. Betting 300 (half the pot) allows me to take down the pot immediately or set up a barrel on the turn if the board develops favorably.

The opponent makes a half-pot continuation bet on a relatively dry Jack-high board. My holding, KTo, has completely missed the flop. I only have King-high with no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw. Continuing here by calling or raising would be highly unprofitable as I have very little equity against the opponent's value and bluffing ranges. Preserving my stack and folding is the most disciplined and optimal action in this spot.

I am on the button with Jack-Six offsuit. While it is tempting to open wide from the button in a three-handed game, J6o is generally too weak to be profitable against the blinds' defending ranges, especially this early in the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
I have 8-2 offsuit in the small blind, which is an extremely weak hand. There is no reason to limp or raise out of position with garbage. Folding is the correct standard play.

I am on the Button with Ace-Queen suited, a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game. I will make a standard 2.5x raise to build the pot and take the initiative pre-flop. This hand plays very well post-flop, and having position allows me to control the action.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. The button has opened, which is typically a wide range. Playing out of position with QTo is disadvantageous, so I prefer to 3-bet to 850 to take control of the pot, define the opponent's range, and potentially win the hand immediately. If called, I maintain the betting lead for the flop.

I am holding 3d 6c, which is a very weak hand. Pro raised from the button and Flashlight 3-bet from the small blind. Given the strength of my hand and being out of position against both opponents, folding is the only sensible move. There is no reason to commit more chips to this pot with one of the worst starting hands in poker.

Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind, AcQc is a premium hand that plays exceptionally well in position. A 4-bet might isolate me against a tighter range that crushes me (such as AK or QQ+), whereas calling keeps the opponent's 3-bet bluffs and weaker value hands (like AJ, AT, or KQ) in the pot. I will call to realize my equity and leverage my positional advantage post-flop.

I have the betting lead after 3-betting pre-flop and now have a flush draw with the Ten of Diamonds on a board of 5d-Jh-7d. The board texture is somewhat coordinated, but I maintain the initiative. A continuation bet is the standard play to represent strength, put pressure on middle-pair hands, and potentially take down the pot or set up future streets if I hit my diamond. Betting approximately 40-50% of the pot is appropriate.

Facing a continuation bet on a wet 5d-Jh-7d board after a pre-flop 3-bet, my AcQc has completely missed. I only have two overcards and no flush draw. Calling here with just Ace-high is too loose against a strong 3-bet range that likely connects with this board or has me crushed with an overpair like QQ-AA. I do not have sufficient equity to float, so folding is the most optimal play to preserve my stack.

The hand 9h2h is a weak suited connector with poor high-card value and weak kicker potential. Even in position on the button, this is a clear fold to avoid playing a negative expected value hand, especially given the tournament context of maximizing stack preservation early.

I am in the Small Blind with KJs (King-Jack of Clubs), a premium hand in a shorthanded or heads-up situation. Since the Button (Flashlight) has folded, the action is on me to either complete, fold, or raise against the Big Blind (Pro). Raising is the standard aggressive play here to take control of the pot and capitalize on the equity of a strong suited broadway hand. A 3x raise to 300 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the Big Blind while maintaining a manageable pot size.

Holding AhKh in the big blind against a small blind open is a premium spot. A 3-bet puts pressure on their likely wide stealing range, builds the pot for value, and gives us the initiative while playing in position post-flop.

I started by opening to 300 with KJs, a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario. Pro's 3-bet to 900 is a standard response. KJs is too strong to fold here, as it has excellent equity and post-flop playability. While 4-betting is an option, calling is often better to keep the pot manageable while out of position and to avoid getting blown off the hand by a 5-bet shove. I will call the 600 more to see the flop.

I am out of position as the caller of a 3-bet. The flop (Th 7s 3s) is relatively disconnected but contains a flush draw. With King-high and two overcards, I have some equity but not enough to lead into the aggressor. I will check to see how Pro reacts to this board. If they bet, I'll have to evaluate the sizing and my potential to improve on the turn.

On a Th 7s 3s board, my AhKh misses completely, though I have two overcards and a backdoor nut flush draw. Since my opponent called a 3-bet pre-flop out of position, their range includes many tens, medium pocket pairs, and spade draws that will not fold to a single continuation bet. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and evaluate the turn card in position while keeping my opponent's missed broadway hands in their range.

The turn brings the 9 of spades, completing a potential flush and adding more straight possibilities. My King-high with no spades has very little value here, and since Pro checked back the flop in a 3-bet pot, their range likely consists of marginal made hands or overcards, some of which may now have a spade. There is no reason to bet and turn my hand into a bluff against a range that could easily call or raise me. I will check to see the river and decide based on Pro's action.

The 9s completes the flush draw and adds 4-to-a-straight possibilities. My AhKh has no spades and very little equity if called. Since Flash checked twice, they might have a marginal made hand, a weak draw, or are trapping with a flush. Betting here as a pure bluff is risky because I have zero redraws to a flush and only six outs to a top pair that might already be drawing dead. Checking back allows me to take a free river card and see if I can spike an Ace or King, or simply get to showdown cheaply against worse Ace-highs.

I am holding King-high (Kc Jc) on a board of Th-7s-3s-9s-6h. Pro, the pre-flop 3-bettor, checked back both the flop and the turn, which strongly suggests they have a hand with some showdown value but are not strong enough to value bet (like a medium pair or Ace-high) or they have completely given up. While King-high has very little showdown value, betting into a range that has checked twice often fails because the opponent is incentivized to call with any pair. Furthermore, I do not have a spade in my hand to use as a blocker for a bluff. Checking is the most prudent play, as it allows me to see the showdown for free or fold if Pro decides to bet.

Flash has checked all three streets, which heavily suggests a marginal one-pair hand (like a small pocket pair, a weak seven, or a nine) or a missed broadway hand (like AQ, AJ, KQ, or QJ). Since I hold Ace-King high, I have decent showdown value against their missed broadway hands. Attempting to bluff at this stage is risky because they may decide to hero-call with a sticky pair or a weak ten. Checking behind allows me to safely realize my equity and get to showdown without risking any more chips.

I am on the button with Kc 5d. In a three-handed game, a King-high hand is often strong enough to open for a steal attempt, especially from the button which grants me positional advantage for the rest of the hand. Although my kicker is weak and the blinds (Pro and Flashlight) have shown aggression in previous hands, a standard raise here is a fundamentally sound play to collect the blinds or take control of the pot. I will raise to 250 chips.

We are in the Small Blind with 5h6h facing a 2.5x open from the Button. Suited connectors can be tempting to play, but calling from the Small Blind is problematic because we do not close the action (the Big Blind could squeeze) and we will be out of position for the entire hand post-flop. 3-betting as a bluff is an option, but we can find better spots with better blockers. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-deuce offsuit, a very weak holding. The opponent on the button raised to 2.5x the big blind. Calling would put me in a position of playing a weak hand out of position against a range that likely dominates mine. Given the stack depths and the poor playability of my cards, folding is the most disciplined and profitable play.
I am on the Button in a 3-handed game with KQo, which is a strong starting hand. Raising to 2.5x (250) to steal the blinds or build a pot in position is standard play.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Three offsuit. This is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raise. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic choice to avoid being put in a tough post-flop spot with a marginal holding.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 2.5x the big blind. While the pot odds to call are roughly 2.7:1, this hand has very poor playability and realization of equity, especially when I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Defending with such a weak hand often leads to difficult spots on the flop. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

K4 offsuit is a weak starting hand, especially in a three-handed game. With two active and aggressive opponents in the blinds, attempting to play this hand will likely lead to being outplayed post-flop or dominated by better kings. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic choice to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 6h, a strong suited connector. The Button has folded. In a head-up situation against the Big Blind, 7h 6h is well above the average holding and plays excellently post-flop. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative, represent strength, and potentially win the blinds immediately. If called, I have a hand that can flop various draws and pairs.
I am dealt 32o in the big blind, which is one of the worst possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a 3x raise from the small blind, this is a trivial fold.

I am on the button with pocket deuces in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening hand from this position to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) which is a consistent sizing for my opening range.

Facing a 3bb raise from the Button, calling from the Small Blind with low suited connectors like 4h 5h is generally unprofitable. We would be out of position post-flop without the betting lead, and the Big Blind could still squeeze us out of the hand. Folding is the safest and most standard play.

Flash is raising from the button, which is a wide opening range. Being in the big blind with Ten-Eight offsuit, I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 into a 450 total pot) to see a flop. Given the early stage of the tournament, defending the big blind with this hand is standard to prevent being pushed around too easily, and I can evaluate my position post-flop.

I am in the big blind out of position on a Jack-high board, holding Ten-Eight offsuit, which has completely missed. With no pair, no draw, and no real equity, leading into the opponent is a weak bluff attempt that will likely be called or raised. Checking is the standard play here to evaluate the opponent's action and control the size of the pot, as I am essentially playing this hand as air.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with pocket deuces on a dry Jack-high board (Js 3h 6c). While my hand is an underpair and vulnerable, the board is very disconnected, making it a good candidate for a continuation bet. Flashlight checked to me, and a bet of approximately half the pot (300 chips) should be enough to fold out many overcards like KQ, AT, or 98 that have significant equity against my deuces. If I am called, I will likely check behind on most turns unless I improve to a set, as my hand's showdown value is marginal.

The flop is Jack-high with no straight or flush draws available that correlate with my Ten-Eight offsuit. Having completely missed the board and holding only Ten-high, I have no equity to justify continuing against a continuation bet of 300 into a 950 pot. Calling would be a losing play as I have no showdown value and minimal potential to improve, so folding is the correct disciplined decision.

On the button with a suited Ace, I have a profitable open-raise opportunity. Raising to 2.5x (250) is a standard sizing that steals the blinds effectively while keeping the pot manageable if called. A6s has good playability post-flop with nut flush potential.

I am in the small blind with King-Nine suited, a hand with decent equity and playability. With the Button opening, calling is a standard approach to realize equity while keeping the pot size manageable, especially since I am out of position. A 3-bet could isolate but risks bloating the pot OOP against a button range, so I will call to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 3h. The Button raised to 250 and the Small Blind called. I need to call 150 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 750. This gives me 5:1 pot odds. While 93s is a weak hand, the price is too good to fold, and the hand has some post-flop playability due to being suited. I will call to see the flop.

I have top pair with King-Nine, which is a respectable hand but vulnerable in a three-way pot, especially being out of position against two opponents. With the flop containing a Jack and a King, there are plenty of combinations that have me beat or have significant equity against me. Leading into two players is risky and could bloat the pot when my kicker is weak. Checking is the most balanced approach, allowing me to control the pot size, evaluate the aggression of my opponents, and decide on a course of action based on their betting behavior.

I have a flush draw with 9h 3h on a 7h Jh Ks board. The pot is 750. The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) is yet to act, and the small blind (Flashlight) has already checked. Checking is the most standard play here to see how the button reacts and to potentially realize my equity cheaply or see a free turn card if everyone checks around.

With the nut flush draw on a K-J-7 flop, I have massive equity. Both opponents checking to me signals weakness. A continuation bet of about half the pot (350) allows me to potentially take down the pot uncontested right now. If called, I have strong equity to hit the nut flush and betting builds the pot for when I hit.

I have top pair with King-Nine on a King-Jack-Seven board. While my kicker is weak and I am out of position against two opponents, the pot odds (350 to win a total pot of 1100) are favorable for a call. Folding top pair to a half-pot continuation bet would be too conservative this early in the hand, and raising is unnecessary given the strength of the board and my position. Calling allows me to see the turn card and evaluate further action while keeping the pot manageable.

I have a flush draw with 9h 3h on a 7h Jh Ks board. The pot is currently 1450 after Pro's bet of 350 and Flashlight's call. I need to call 350, which gives me pot odds of approximately 4:1 (350 to win 1450). With 9 outs to a flush, I have about a 19% chance of hitting on the turn and 35% by the river. The price is right to see a turn card, especially since I am closing the action on this street and have the potential to win a larger pot if I hit my flush. Therefore, calling is the most profitable decision.

The board has brought a third heart, creating significant flush potential. My top pair is still a decent holding, but betting out of position against two opponents would likely just isolate myself against better hands or get called by better draws. Checking allows me to re-evaluate based on the action behind me and keep the pot manageable.

I have completed a 9-high flush on the turn. The pot is currently 1800. The small blind (Flashlight) has checked to me. While I have a strong hand, leading out now might fold out weaker hands like Kx or Jx that would otherwise bet if checked to. By checking, I allow the button (Pro), who was the pre-flop and flop aggressor, the opportunity to continue betting his range, including semi-bluffs or top-pair hands. If the button bets and the small blind folds or calls, I can then decide whether to check-raise for value or just call to keep his bluffs in. This also helps control the pot size in case someone has already turned a higher flush with the Ah or Qh.

The turn gives me the nut flush. Both opponents checked, and after they both called the flop, their ranges likely include top pairs (Kx), middle pairs (Jx), two pairs, sets, or worse flushes that got there. I have the absolute nuts and need to bet for value and to build the pot. A bet of 1200 (around 2/3 pot) extracts maximum value from strong made hands and worse flushes that will have a hard time folding.

I am facing a significant bet of 1,200 into a three-way pot on a very wet board with three hearts. While I have top pair (Kings), my kicker is weak (Nine), and I have no flush draw or straight draw to support my hand. A bet into two players on this texture strongly suggests the opponent has either a made hand better than mine (like a set, two-pair, or a flush) or is semi-bluffing with a significant draw. Calling here is too risky given the likelihood of being behind and the potential for the Big Blind to act behind me. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I have turned a 9-high flush. The pre-flop and flop aggressor (Pro) has bet 1200 into a pot of 1800 after the third heart landed. While I have a strong hand, a 9-high flush is vulnerable to higher flushes (specifically those containing the Ah, Qh, or Th). By calling instead of raising, I control the size of the pot and allow Pro to continue betting with worse hands like sets, two pair, or even semi-bluffs. Raising might only get called by better hands and fold out the hands I currently beat. I will call to see the river and re-evaluate.

I have a 9-high flush on a board that didn't pair on the river (7h-Jh-Ks-5h-4d). I am out of position against Pro, who has been the aggressor throughout the hand. While my flush is strong, the Ah, Qh, and Th of hearts would beat me. On the turn, Pro bet 1200 into 1800 after the third heart arrived, which indicates significant strength or a high-equity semi-bluff. By checking the river, I allow Pro the opportunity to bet his missed draws or value-bet worse hands like top pair, two pair, or sets. If I lead out, I might fold out his bluffs and only get called or raised by hands that beat me (higher flushes). Therefore, checking with the intention of calling a reasonable bet is the most balanced and profitable play.

I have the absolute nut flush on a safe river card that changes nothing. Flash check-called my turn bet, which suggests they have a strong hand like a worse flush, a set, two pair, or a very stubborn King. Since they checked to me again, I need to extract maximum value. Betting around 60% of the pot (2500 chips) is a solid value bet sizing that allows worse flushes to snap-call while still giving strong one-pair or two-pair hands the correct price to potentially talk themselves into a hero call.

The board is 7h Jh Ks 5h 4d and I hold the 9h 3h for a 9-high flush. Pro has been the aggressor on every street, betting 350 on the flop, 1200 on the turn when the flush completed, and now 2500 on the river. While a 9-high flush is a strong hand, it is vulnerable to the Ah, Qh, and Th. However, Pro's range also includes sets (KK, JJ, 77), two pairs (KJ, K7, J7), and potential blushes or semi-bluffs with the Ah. Given the pot odds (2500 to win 6700, requiring ~27% equity), folding a flush on an unpaired board is too exploitable. Raising is risky because I likely only get called by the higher flushes that beat me. Therefore, calling is the most prudent action to maximize value against his non-flush value bets and bluffs while minimizing loss against his nutted hands.

Jack-Deuce offsuit is an extremely weak starting hand with poor playability and no real equity. As the button, I have the advantage of position, but this hand is too dominated to justify an open or a call. Folding is the standard, disciplined play to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Ten-Five offsuit is an extremely weak hand to play out of position, even in a small blind vs big blind situation. With the big blind holding a significant chip lead and showing aggressive tendencies in previous hands, completing the bet or raising with this hand is unlikely to be profitable in the long run. Folding is the most disciplined action to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am currently the short stack at the table with 54.5 BBs. Holding 9c 6h on the button, this is a very weak starting hand. While button aggression is important in 3-handed play, 96o does not offer enough playability or equity to justify an open-raise against two deeper stacks, especially considering Pro's aggressive history in previous hands. I will wait for a better spot to commit chips.

Qh 2d is a weak, disconnected offsuit hand. Even though it's folded to me in the small blind, playing out of position against the big blind with this hand is unprofitable. Folding is the standard and safest play.
6s 8d is a weak offsuit one-gapper. From the Button, there's no reason to invest chips with this holding. Folding pre-flop is the standard and safest play.

I am in the small blind with Ten-Seven offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Since the button (Pro) has already folded, I am only facing the big blind (Flash). However, there is no strategic incentive to play such a weak hand out of position against the big blind, especially given the range advantage they hold by being the big blind. The best course of action is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button in a three-handed game, which allows for a wider opening range to steal the blinds. Although Queen-Seven offsuit is a marginal holding, it is strong enough to play in this position given that no other players have acted. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the small and big blinds and potentially take down the pot immediately.

In a three-handed game, Ace-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard button raise. While 3-betting is a viable aggressive option, calling allows me to see a flop and keep the pot manageable out of position. It also prevents over-committing against a potentially stronger range from the button or a squeeze from the big blind's massive stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, calling out of position with this hand is a losing long-term play despite the pot odds. Folding is the standard and correct decision.

On this flop of 2c-7c-8s, my Ace-Nine offsuit has missed completely. I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. Checking is the standard play here to see how the button reacts. I have some showdown value with Ace-high, but I am not strong enough to lead out into the initial raiser on a board that could easily connect with their range or contain overpairs.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have flopped middle pair with a Queen kicker. The board is slightly coordinated with a club flush draw, so checking back to realize equity is an option, but a continuation bet is the standard play to gain information and protect against overcards. I will bet 350, which is half the pot, to charge any draws and see how my opponent reacts.

On this board of 2c-7c-8s, my Ace-Nine offsuit has no direct connection and I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. While I have Ace-high and a backdoor straight draw, Flashlight's continuation bet is standard for a button opener and could represent anything from a total air bluff to a strong pair or a set. Calling 350 into a 1050 pot offers decent odds, but being out of position makes it extremely difficult to realize my equity on the turn and river if I don't improve. Given that I am the shortest stack at the table, I prefer to fold here and preserve my chips for a more favorable situation rather than calling down with a marginal hand and potentially facing larger bets on future streets.

I am on the button with 73s. While standard button opening ranges in 3-handed play are quite wide, 73s is near the bottom of the range and generally considered a fold unless the blinds are playing extremely tight. Both Pro and Flashlight have shown a willingness to contest pots in previous hands. Given my stack size of 52 big blinds, there is no need to force a steal with such a weak hand.

With Q6o in the Small Blind against the Big Blind, our hand is too weak to limp or raise profitably, especially out of position. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play to conserve our stack.

Pocket Jacks is a very strong starting hand. Being on the Button, raising is mandatory to build the pot for value, protect our equity, and establish control. A raise to 2.5x (250 chips) is my standard open size, keeping my range balanced.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Jack suited, which is a strong hand that plays well against a wide button opening range. Given that Pro is the opener on the button, their range is likely quite wide, and 3-betting allows me to take control of the hand, define my range, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or gain initiative for post-flop play. A 3-bet to 900 is a standard, effective sizing to isolate the opponent and build a pot with a hand that has good equity.

I am in the Big Blind with K8o. The Button (Pro) raised to 250, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) 3-bet to 900. K8o is a weak hand to defend against a 3-bet, as it is likely dominated by the Small Blind's range (which often includes hands like AK, KQ, or high pairs). Furthermore, calling 800 chips into this pot with a marginal hand and the possibility of the original raiser 4-betting or calling behind makes this a high-risk, low-reward situation. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Flashlight 3-bet from the Small Blind to 900. We hold pocket Jacks on the Button, which is a premium hand but potentially vulnerable if we face a 5-bet shove from QQ+ or AK. By calling the 3-bet in position, we keep their 3-betting bluffs and weaker value hands (like AQ, AJ, TT, 99) in the pot while maintaining our positional advantage post-flop. A 4-bet might isolate us against a tighter range that has us crushed. Therefore, calling is the most balanced and optimal play.

The board [As, 8s, 9d] is quite wet and favors the opponent's calling range, which often includes pocket pairs and suited connectors that hit this texture well. As I am out of position and only hold Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw, betting into this board is high-risk with low reward. I will check to control the pot size and evaluate the opponent's next move.

With an Ace on the flop, my pocket Jacks lose a significant amount of value against a pre-flop 3-bettor's range. Flashlight's check could indicate pot control with a hand like AK/AQ, a trap with a monster, or giving up with missed broadways and underpairs like TT/QQ/KK. If I bet, worse hands will likely fold and better hands will call or raise. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and keep his bluffs in the hand for later streets.

The board has been checked through on both the flop and the turn, which indicates that both players are likely holding marginal hands or are cautious. Since I hold Queen-high with no pair or draw on an A-high board, my showdown value is extremely low. Betting now would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to succeed, as it would be difficult to represent an Ace after checking the previous streets. Checking is the most prudent action as it allows me to control the pot size and reach the showdown at no further cost, or fold if the opponent makes a significant bet.

Flashlight has checked twice, which could indicate a weak Ace, a higher pocket pair like KK/QQ, a lower pair like TT, or a missed draw. While we might have the best hand against some of his range, betting here mostly gets called by better hands (like Ax, KK, QQ) and folds out worse hands. Checking again keeps the pot small, controls risk, and allows us to realize our showdown value or evaluate the river.

The board has three spades and an Ace. My Jack-high is extremely weak and cannot beat any pair or Ace that the opponent might have. Since the opponent checked behind on the flop and turn, their range is likely capped at marginal hands or missed draws. Betting at this point would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from any hand that has showdown value, and it risks losing more chips. Checking is the most prudent action to reach the showdown at no additional cost.

Flashlight has checked all three post-flop streets, showing significant passivity. My pocket Jacks have decent showdown value against missed broadway hands (like KQ or QJ) and lower pairs like TT. However, betting here is extremely thin; worse hands will almost always fold, and I will only get called by better hands such as a trapped Ax, KK, QQ, or a rivered flush. Checking behind guarantees that we see a free showdown and realize our equity without unnecessary risk.

78o is a weak hand with poor equity, especially when playing against strong opponents. While being on the button provides a positional advantage, the hand does not merit a raise as a blind steal, and calling out of position relative to the big blind is unnecessary. I will fold to conserve chips and wait for a stronger starting hand.

With Flashlight folding on the button, I am heads-up against the big blind (Pro). Queen-eight offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the small blind in a three-handed game. Raising to 300 allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately or set up a post-flop play with positional advantage or initiative.

Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, 69 suited is a bit too weak and disconnected to defend, even with position. Given the lack of antes and the wide but aggressive tendencies of the opponent, folding is the most solid and standard choice to preserve the stack.

I am on the button with K3s. In a 3-handed game, K3s is a strong enough hand to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down pre-flop. My stack is the shortest at the table, so I need to look for opportunities to pick up blinds and antes. A standard 3x open raise is appropriate here.
K4o is a weak offsuit king that plays very poorly out of position. Facing a 3x open from the button, folding is the standard and correct play.

Flash raised from the button to 300, and the small blind folded. I am in the big blind with Td-9s. I am getting favorable pot odds (2.25:1) to call, and Td-9s is a hand with decent playability post-flop. Given the stack depth and the current situation, calling to see a flop is the most standard and profitable play.

I am out of position in the big blind against the button on an A-high, monotone board. My hand (Td-9s) has no hearts, no pair, and no significant equity. Since my opponent is the pre-flop aggressor and in position, checking is the standard play to gauge their strength. I will look to check-fold if they continue with a significant bet, as this texture hits the button's range very well.

I was the pre-flop aggressor, but this flop is quite coordinated with three hearts and an Ace. I hold [3s, Ks], meaning I have no hearts and no pair. While a continuation bet could represent an Ace or a high heart, Flashlight's range in the big blind is likely to contain many hands that connected with this board or have a heart draw. Given I am the short stack and have zero equity on this board, checking back is the most prudent play to see a free turn and avoid bloating a pot with a hand that currently has very little chance of winning a showdown.

The board is monotone with a flush draw possible, and I hold Td-9s with no hearts, no pair, and no draw. My hand has effectively zero equity. Since both players checked the flop, the opponent's range is likely weak or capped. Betting here would be a pure bluff into a dry pot with no fold equity against better hands. Checking is the most standard play to keep the pot small and hopefully reach a showdown, or simply check-fold if the opponent leads out on the river.

Flashlight has checked twice on a board that features three hearts and an Ace. My pre-flop raise allows me to represent a strong range, and my flop check can be interpreted as pot control with an Ace or a slow-play with a flush. Since Flashlight has shown weakness by checking again on a blank turn, a delayed continuation bet is likely to fold out their marginal holdings or air. I will bet around 60% of the pot to take it down now.

The board is 4h-Ah-2h-7s and I hold Td-9s. I have no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw. My opponent (Flash) checked the flop but is now betting 400 on the turn. Given that an Ace is on the board and there are two hearts, my hand has virtually no showdown value and very little equity against a continuing range. Since I am out of position and have no realistic path to winning the pot, folding is the most disciplined and strategic decision to preserve my stack.
I am on the button with 2h 5d, which is an extremely weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand. Even though I have position, this is a clear fold pre-flop.

I am in the small blind with a weak starting hand (Ten-Five offsuit). Since the button (Pro) folded, I am heads-up against the big blind (Flash). Given the poor relative strength and playability of my hand, it is mathematically optimal to fold and preserve my stack rather than committing more chips to play a marginal hand out of position.

I have been dealt 7d 2s, which is statistically the worst possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Even with the positional advantage of being on the button in a 3-handed game, there is no strategic justification for playing this hand. I should fold to conserve my stack and wait for a more playable hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 5s. The Button (Flashlight) has folded, leaving just me and the Big Blind (Pro). 85s is a decent speculative hand in a 3-handed game. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to attempt to take down the blinds and keep the initiative. If Pro calls, the hand has some post-flop potential with its flush and straight possibilities, although I will be out of position.
I am dealt 92o, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the small blind, there is absolutely no reason to play this hand, so I will fold.

I am on the button with Q9o, which is a standard opening hand in a 3-handed game. I have 57 big blinds, and a 3x open (300 chips) has been my consistent sizing throughout the tournament so far. Both Pro and Flashlight are relatively deep, but playing in position allows me to control the pot. Q9o is strong enough to steal the blinds or play post-flop if called.

I am dealt 3c 8s in the Small Blind, which is a very weak and unplayable offsuit hand. Facing a standard 3x raise from the Button, the most optimal and profitable action is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

Defending the big blind with Jack-Ten offsuit against a button raise is a standard, profitable play. With decent equity and sufficient pot odds, calling is the most balanced approach to keep the pot manageable and see the flop in position.

The board (5c-2h-5h) is dry and unpaired for my hand, and my Jack-high holds no equity against the opponent's range. As the out-of-position player, I must check to see how the pre-flop aggressor proceeds. This allows me to control the pot size and avoid committing chips with a weak hand.

On a board of 5c 2h 5h, as the pre-flop aggressor on the button, I have a range advantage. This board texture favors my opening range which includes more high cards and pairs, though it is quite dry. Flashlight checked to me, which is standard. I hold Queen-high (Qd 9c), which has some showdown value against complete air but little else. A continuation bet here serves to take down the pot immediately, as it is difficult for Flashlight to have connected strongly with this board unless they have a Five or a small pocket pair. I'll bet small (roughly 40% of the pot) to deny equity to their random overcards and capitalize on my positional advantage.

On a dry, paired board of 5c-2h-5h, my Jack-Ten high has very little equity. While the pot odds are tempting, calling with Jack-high against a continuation bet from the button is a losing proposition in the long run, as I am behind any pair, any ace-high, and many other hands in the opponent's range. Preserving my stack for a more favorable situation is the disciplined choice.
69o is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Even from the button, it's not strong enough to open-raise or call, so folding is the standard and correct play.

K5o is a weak hand to play, even in a blind-versus-blind situation. Since I have already posted the small blind and it costs 50 more to complete, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack and avoid playing a marginal hand out of position post-flop.

I hold a pocket pair of deuces on the button. This is a strong starting hand that plays well in position. I want to build a pot or take down the blinds immediately. A 2.5x open-raise is a standard and effective sizing to exert pressure and maintain the initiative.

I am in the Small Blind with JTo. Flashlight on the Button has opened for 250. JTo is a solid hand in a 3-way game, especially against a Button raise which typically represents a wide range. While I could 3-bet to try and take the pot immediately or isolate, calling is a standard and safe play to see a flop with a hand that has good connectivity and high-card value. The pot is offering 2:1 on a call (200 to win a total of 400). I will call and see how the flop develops.

With A3o in the Big Blind facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, we are out of position with a marginal, easily dominated hand. Although we are getting 4:1 pot odds, A3o plays very poorly multiway post-flop and our equity realization will be low. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play to preserve our chip lead.

I am in the Small Blind with an open-ended straight draw (9-high straight) on a board of 8s-6s-7h. I am out of position against Flashlight, the pre-flop raiser. Standard play in this situation is to check to the aggressor to see how they react. If they bet, I have enough equity to continue, but I want to keep the pot manageable or see if I can see a turn card for free.

The board [8s, 6s, 7h] is extremely coordinated and hits the small blind's calling range well. My pocket deuces have almost no equity against potential straight draws, flush draws, or any pair higher than a deuce. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands, and I do not have enough value to justify a bet on such a wet board. Checking allows me to keep the pot small and evaluate the turn while essentially giving up on this hand unless I improve, which is unlikely.

The turn Queen of Spades is a significant card. It completes potential flushes and gives me a flush draw (holding the Jack of Spades) to go along with my open-ended straight draw (totaling 15 outs: four 9s, four 5s, and the remaining 7 spades). Since Flashlight checked back the flop, they likely don't have a dominant hand like an overpair or a set. I will check to see how the button reacts. My hand has immense drawing equity, making it a strong candidate for check-calling or seeing a free river if Flashlight checks back again.

The turn card, the Queen of Spades, completes a potential flush and further solidifies the coordinated nature of the board. My pocket deuces have almost zero showdown value and are essentially a bluff catcher that cannot even catch a bluff if the opponent has any pair higher than a deuce or a single spade. Betting would only isolate me against hands that beat me. Checking behind is the optimal play to maintain pot control and minimize further investment in a hand that is likely beaten.

On this river, the board has paired with the Queen of Hearts, and there are three spades on the board. My hand (Jack-high) has failed to improve to a straight or a flush. Since Flashlight checked back both the flop and the turn, their range is likely capped to marginal made hands like mid-pairs (7x, 8x) or Ace-high. While I could attempt a bluff to represent a Queen or a slow-played flush, the pot is relatively small (600) and it's risky to bluff into a range that contains many hands with showdown value. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown, though I expect to lose to most of the hands Flashlight would reach this point with.

I am on the river with pocket deuces on a board of 8s-6s-7h-Qs-Qh. My hand has no showdown value and cannot beat any pair or better. The opponent checked, and betting would be a thin and ineffective bluff against a range that includes flush draws and pairs. Checking behind is the most sensible play to minimize losses and reach showdown to see if my hand is somehow good (though unlikely) or simply fold without investing more chips.

I am on the button with Q9o in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand. I have consistently been raising to 300 (3x the big blind) from the button in previous hands to take control of the pot and leverage my position. I will continue this strategy here to put pressure on the blinds.

Ten-Six offsuit is a very weak, disconnected hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Button while in the Small Blind, we will be out of position post-flop with a very marginal holding. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten suited, which is a strong, highly playable hand against a button opening range. Given the pot odds and the strength of the cards, defending by calling is a standard and optimal strategy, allowing me to see a flop while keeping the pot size under control out of position.

I am facing an Ace-high board with middle pair (Tens). While I have showdown value, the board texture is wet, and an Ace is squarely in my opponent's opening range. Leading out as the pre-flop caller is generally weak in this spot. Checking allows me to evaluate my opponent's continuation bet and pot control, keeping the pot manageable while playing out of position.

I raised pre-flop and the flop came Ace-high, which strongly favors my perceived range as the aggressor. Although I only have Queen high, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack). Flashlight checked to me, which is standard for the Big Blind. I will place a continuation bet of 300, which is consistent with my previous post-flop aggression. This bet aims to fold out hands like small pairs or air that Flashlight might have called with pre-flop, while leveraging the presence of the Ace on the board.

I have middle pair (Tens) on an Ace-high board. Flash's c-bet of 300 into a 650 pot is a standard sizing that could represent an Ace, a draw, or a weaker hand. Folding here is too conservative given my relative hand strength. Raising turns my hand into a bluff or value-cuts me against better holdings. Calling is the most prudent decision to keep the pot size manageable and see how the turn develops.

The turn card, the 5 of hearts, increases the likelihood of a flush draw or a gutshot straight draw, making the board texture significantly more dangerous. As the out-of-position player with middle pair, checking is the most prudent strategic decision. It allows me to maintain pot control, re-evaluate based on Flash's reaction, and avoid bloating the pot against a range that potentially includes top pair or better. If Flash checks behind, I get to see a river for free; if Flash bets, I can evaluate the sizing relative to my stack and pot odds.

The turn card (5h) completes a potential flush and doesn't improve my Queen-high hand or my gutshot straight draw. Flashlight called my flop bet, which suggests they have a piece of the board, such as an Ace, a Ten, or a heart draw. Since the heart draw has now potentially arrived and I have no heart in my hand to block their range, betting again is risky. Checking back allows me to see a free river card and potentially realize my equity if a Jack falls, or simply reach showdown cheaply if the opponent is also weak.

I hold a pair of Tens on a board where an Ace and multiple draws are present. My opponent, Flash, checked the turn after c-betting the flop, which indicates a range that is likely capped, possibly containing weak pairs or missed draws. Betting for value is ineffective because I would likely only be called by better hands (like an Ace or a set), and betting as a bluff doesn't make sense since my hand has showdown value. Checking is the optimal line to reach showdown and capitalize on potential bluffs from Flash or win against other weak pairs in his range.

The river board is 8h-Ah-Ts-5h-7c and I hold Queen-high (Qc 9s). Flashlight called my flop continuation bet, which suggests they have a piece of the board, likely an Ace or a Ten. The turn checked through after the third heart appeared, and Flashlight has checked again on the river. While Queen-high has zero showdown value, a bluff is unlikely to fold out an Ace, and my turn check makes it difficult to credibly represent the flush. Therefore, I will check behind and concede the pot rather than risking more chips on a low-probability bluff.

T6 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, no flush potential, and low high-card value. Even from the Button, it is not strong enough to warrant an open raise to steal the blinds. Folding is the standard and optimal play.

Pro has folded on the button, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind. Holding King-Seven suited, I have a playable hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Completing the small blind to see a flop is a standard and low-risk approach to playing this pot against the Big Blind.

Flashlight completed from the small blind after Pro folded. With Queen-Nine offsuit in the big blind, I have a hand with decent high-card value but not strong enough to warrant a raise against a potential limp-trap or to build a large pot out of position. I will check to see the flop for free and evaluate on the next street.

I am in the small blind with middle pair (pairing my seven). The board is somewhat connected with potential flush and straight draws. As I am first to act on the flop, checking is the standard play to keep the pot manageable and allow me to evaluate the opponent's action. This prevents me from bloated the pot with a marginal hand against a potentially stronger range, while allowing me to call a reasonable bet or fold if the pressure becomes too high.

I have two overcards (Queen and Nine) and a gutshot straight draw (a 6 makes a straight: 4-6-7-8-9). Flashlight checked from the small blind in a limped pot, which often indicates a weak range. While I could bet to try and take it down, checking back allows me to see a free turn card and realize my equity without risking chips on a semi-bluff into a board that could easily have connected with a small blind's limping range (which contains many middle cards).

I am in the small blind with a pair of sevens and a club flush draw. The pot is relatively small at 200 chips. Both players have checked through the flop and the turn, which indicates neither of us has hit a monster hand yet. By checking again, I keep the pot manageable and see the river card for free, potentially completing my flush draw or holding up with my pair of sevens at showdown without having to commit unnecessary chips.

On the turn, the Ten of Clubs gives me an open-ended straight draw (any Jack or Six completes a straight) to go along with my two overcards (Queen and Nine). Flashlight has checked twice now, indicating a very weak range or a marginal hand that wants to see the river cheaply. While I could bet as a semi-bluff, checking back is the most prudent play in this small limped pot. It allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially win a larger pot if I hit one of my 8 straight outs or even pair up on the river.

I have a pair of sevens and have checked down to the river. The opponent has also checked through the flop, turn, and river. Given that I have showdown value and the pot is small (200), checking is the optimal play to reach showdown. Betting would likely only be called by better hands or would turn my hand into an unnecessary bluff, and I have enough showdown value to potentially win against a random missed draw or a weaker pair.

The board is 4-7-8-T-2 and I hold Queen-high (Q9). Flashlight limped from the small blind and has checked every street. This suggests a very weak range. While my Queen-high might lose to Ace-high or any small pair, there is very little value in bluffing into a tiny 200-chip pot. I will check back and take my showdown value for free, as my hand may still be the best among two very weak ranges.

Pocket Kings (Kh Ks) is an extremely strong starting hand. In this 3-handed situation from the button, I should open the betting to build the pot and attempt to isolate one of the blinds. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing to maximize value while gauging the opponents' reactions.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Six offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for a 3x raise. Q6o is a weak hand to play out of position, even in a three-handed game. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop against two opponents (if the Big Blind also calls) or one opponent while being out of position. Given my current stack and the strength of the hand, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

With 54s in the Big Blind facing a 3x open from the Button, we are getting decent pot odds but will have to play a marginal suited connector out of position. While deep stacks provide some implied odds, defending with such low connectors out of position often leads to tough spots where it's hard to realize our equity. I prefer to fold and wait for better spots or hands that can be more aggressively 3-bet or played with better playability.

I am on the button with a pocket pair (5h 5c). In a three-handed game, pocket fives are a strong starting hand. Raising to 300 is my standard opening size, which maintains consistency with my previous play and puts pressure on the blinds. If called, I have a hand that can flop well (set mining) or potentially win at showdown if the board remains low.

T9o is a weak offsuit connector. Facing a raise from the Button and being out of position in the Small Blind with the Big Blind still to act, playing this hand is unprofitable. Folding is the standard and safest play.

I hold Ace-Jack suited, which is a strong hand that dominates many of the Button's opening range. Given that the opponent has been aggressive, a 3-bet is the best way to take control of the hand, define my range, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. Raising to 950 is a standard, effective sizing for a 3-bet out of the big blind.

I am facing a 3-bet from Flashlight in the Big Blind after my Button open. With pocket fives (55), I am in a difficult spot. The effective stack is 4800, and the call is 650. The stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) if I call would be approximately 2.0, which leaves very little room for post-flop maneuvering unless I flop a set. The 'implied odds' for set mining are not favorable here, as I only have about 7 times the call amount remaining in my stack (4500/650). Against a standard 3-bet range in a 3-handed game, 55 is often a flip at best or dominated by higher pairs. Given the stack depths and the aggressive sizing from the opponent, folding is the most disciplined move to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the Button with Ad Kh, a premium starting hand. Being first to act, a standard open raise of 2.5x the big blind (250) is optimal to build the pot, take control, and extract value or isolate the blinds.

Q5s is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position from the small blind, especially against a button raise. Given my stack size and the positional disadvantage, it is strategically sound to fold and wait for a more favorable hand to engage with.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit (Q2o). The Button (Pro) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind has folded. While the pot odds are somewhat tempting (150 to call into a pot of 400), Q2o is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position against the chip leader. Given my stack size of 47 big blinds, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak starting hand with little potential for value or strong showdown equity. Despite being on the button, this hand does not justify an investment of chips to enter the pot. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve stack health.

I am in the Small Blind with 3h 2d, which is widely considered the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Although it only costs 50 more to call and see a flop after the button folded, this hand has extremely poor equity and post-flop playability. Given my stack size of 4650, I should preserve my chips for more favorable situations rather than bleeding them away on marginal or sub-marginal hands. I will fold.

I am on the button with K7o. In a three-handed game, this is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds. My stack is the shortest at the table, but still healthy enough (46 big blinds) to play standard poker. I will raise to my usual sizing of 300 to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage.
With 9-3 offsuit in the Small Blind facing a raise from the Button, this is an incredibly weak hand and an easy fold. There's no reason to invest any more chips.
K3o is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop, even with the pot odds provided. Folding is the disciplined play here to conserve my stack for better opportunities.
J2o is a very weak starting hand and unplayable in most situations. Since I am first to act on the button with such a weak hand, folding is the best and standard play.

I am in the small blind with 4-3 suited, a speculative hand that is worth playing when the button has folded. Completing to the big blind is standard in this heads-up situation to see a flop with an active opponent, as my pot odds (calling 50 to win 150) are favourable. I will call to complete the bet.

I am in the Big Blind with 4c 5s. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has limped in after the Button folded. While I could attempt to take the pot down now with a raise, my hand is quite weak and I have a relatively short stack compared to my opponents. Checking allows me to see a free flop and realize what little equity I have without risking more chips out of position.

The board is monotone with three spades (9s, 6s, Qs). I hold 4d 3d, giving me no spades and no pair. This board texture heavily favors the Big Blind's defending range, and I have no equity. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot small, evaluate the opponent's action, and be prepared to fold to any meaningful bet.

I have 4c 5s on a board of 9s 6s Qs. This is a monotone board and I hold no spades, no pairs, and no draws. Flashlight checked from the small blind. Given the lack of equity in my hand and the coordinated nature of the board, there is no merit in betting as a bluff here. Checking is the most sensible play to see a free turn card.

The turn is the 6h, pairing the board and keeping the spade flush draw open. I have 4-high with no spades and no pair. My opponent, Flash, has checked both the flop and the turn, which shows weakness. However, I have absolutely no showdown value or equity. Checking is the only standard play here to keep the pot small and see the river for free, though I will be prepared to fold to any bet on the river.

The board is highly coordinated with three spades and a pair of sixes. I have no pair, no draw, and no spades. Since Flashlight checked again, there is no reason to put chips into the pot with zero equity. Checking to see the river for free.

The board is monotone with four spades (9s, 6s, Qs, As) and a paired six. I hold 4d 3d, meaning I have absolutely no piece of this board and no showdown value. Both my opponent and I have checked down every street. With zero equity, betting is pointless as it would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to force a fold given the pot size, while checking is free and allows me to reach showdown to see if my 4-high is somehow good (extremely unlikely) or just lose the minimum if my opponent decides to bet. Checking is the standard and correct play.

On the river, the board shows four spades (As Qs 9s 6s) and a pair of sixes. I hold the 5 of spades, which gives me a 5-high flush. While this is technically a flush, it is very low on a board with four spades. Flashlight has checked through every street after limping pre-flop. There is very little value in betting here, as any hand that calls me likely has a higher spade. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and reach showdown for free with my marginal flush.

Queen-Three offsuit is a very weak starting hand with little potential for value. Given my position on the button, playing such garbage hands leads to difficult post-flop situations. It is optimal to fold and wait for better cards.

I have been dealt pocket Aces (Ah Ad) in the Small Blind. Flashlight has folded on the button. I want to build a pot with the strongest possible starting hand while maintaining my standard raising size to avoid telegraphing my hand strength. My usual open from the Small Blind or Button has been 300 chips (3x the big blind). I will raise to 300 to induce a call or a 3-bet from Pro in the Big Blind.

Facing a 3x open from the Small Blind, I have pocket twos in the Big Blind. While 22 is the lowest pair, I am in position post-flop and the effective stack depth (Flash has 4600 behind) gives me decent implied odds to set-mine. Calling is a profitable play here.

I have pocket Aces, an overpair to the board of K-6-J. This is a very strong hand, especially in a head-up pot against the Big Blind. I want to bet for value and to build the pot, targeting hands like K-x, J-x, and straight draws like Q-T. A bet of 300 chips (half the pot) is consistent with my previous continuation betting style and should induce calls from many worse hands in Pro's range.

The flop comes King, Jack, Six, which hits the pre-flop raiser's range very hard. Facing a continuation bet of half the pot, my pocket twos are completely unprotected and have almost no equity against any King, Jack, or higher pocket pair. With no draws and very slim chances of hitting a set on the turn, folding is the only correct play here.
I am on the button with Ace-Eight suited, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I will make a standard opening raise of 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand from position.

I am dealt Jack-Two offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a pre-flop raise from the Button, and being out of position in the Small Blind, calling or raising here has severe negative expected value. The best and only reasonable decision is to fold.

Pocket Tens are a very strong hand in this spot, especially against a button open range. Simply calling lets the button realize their equity cheaply and invites them to play aggressively post-flop. A 3-bet to 900 is the standard play to build the pot, assert control, and get value from their wider opening range.

I opened to 300 with Ace-Eight suited from the button, and the Big Blind (Flashlight) has 3-bet to 900. Ace-Eight suited is a strong hand in three-handed play, but Flashlight has shown aggression in previous hands. I am in position and have a hand with good playability and some nut potential. While I could 4-bet, calling allows me to see a flop in position and keep the pot manageable if I miss. Given the stack sizes and the 3-bet sizing, calling is a standard and solid play to utilize my positional advantage.

The board texture of K-J-3 with a diamond flush draw is challenging for pocket Tens, but I am the pre-flop aggressor in a 3-bet pot. Checking here would signal weakness and allow the opponent to realize their equity or take control of the hand. A continuation bet of approximately 35-40% of the pot is standard here to define ranges and maintain initiative, forcing the opponent to fold weaker hands or continue only if they have connected with the King or Jack. If I face significant resistance, I can reassess, but leading out is the most strategic play to control the pot.

I missed this flop completely with my Ace-Eight of clubs. The board is heavy with high cards (King and Jack) and a diamond draw, which likely hit the range of Flashlight after they 3-bet pre-flop from the Big Blind. With only Ace high and no real drawing potential on this texture, calling a continuation bet of 700 is not profitable. I should fold here to preserve my remaining 4,300 chips and look for a more favorable situation.

Ten-Nine offsuit is a marginal hand to open from the button. While it has some playability, it is generally safer to fold offsuit connectors this weak, especially considering we have a healthy stack and do not need to force the action.

With Ace-Jack offsuit in the small blind and the button having folded, I have a strong heads-up hand. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the big blind and allows me to take the initiative pre-flop. This is a standard opening size to potentially take down the blinds or play the hand in position post-flop.

I have been dealt pocket Aces, the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Flashlight has raised from the small blind to 300. With a stack of 4200, I need to maximize value and build the pot immediately. A 3-bet to 900 is a standard and effective sizing to either take the pot down now or set up a profitable post-flop situation where I can potentially commit my stack.

With Ace-Jack offsuit, I have a strong hand that holds up well against a wide 3-bet range from the Big Blind. Given the stack depths—Flash has a shorter stack of 3400—calling the 600 raise to see a flop is a solid approach. It keeps the pot manageable, avoids bloating it unnecessarily before seeing the community cards, and allows me to re-evaluate on the flop while maintaining a significant stack advantage over my opponent.

The flop of 5c-8s-8c is not particularly favorable for my Ad-Jh offsuit. Having called a 3-bet pre-flop, my opponent's range is likely strong, containing many hands that either connected with this board or have me dominated with higher pairs. Since I am first to act, checking is the most prudent strategy. I have no pair and no significant draw, so betting would essentially be a speculative bluff into a range that is likely to continue. Checking allows me to re-evaluate based on the opponent's action, keep the pot small, and potentially realize equity if the hand goes to showdown cheaply.

I have pocket Aces on a board of 5c-8s-8c, giving me a very strong overpair. Flashlight called my pre-flop 3-bet and has checked to me on the flop. I need to bet for value and to protect against potential draws, such as clubs or straight draws. A bet of 600 into the 1800 pot (1/3 pot) is a standard continuation bet that extracts value from mid-pairs (99-JJ) and draws while keeping the pot manageable for my stack size.

The pot is currently 2400, and I need to call 600 to continue. This provides me with excellent pot odds of 4:1. With Ace-high and two overs on a relatively dry board of 5-8-8, I have enough equity to justify a call, especially since my opponent's stack is short and this could be a standard continuation bet. Calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to see a turn card, where I might improve or face a more clear decision.

The board texture is 5c-8s-8c-9s. I am out of position against an opponent who 3-bet pre-flop and continued on the flop, signaling significant strength.
