In a blind-vs-blind situation with Flashlight folding from the button, Ace-Five offsuit is a strong hand to open with. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the Big Blind. This allows me to win the blinds immediately or play the hand with initiative if called.
Facing a 3x raise from the small blind, my 86o is too weak and disconnected to defend. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am on the button with 8c 6c, a respectable suited connector that plays well in position. The blinds are 50/100, and it currently costs 100 to call (the big blind). Raising is usually the preferred play on the button to seize the initiative and potentially take the blinds, but given my stack of 6950 and the relatively conservative play seen in some previous hands, a standard raise to 300 is a good way to apply pressure and build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop potential.

I am in the Small Blind with 4h3h, a very low suited connector. Facing a raise from the Button, playing this hand out of position is a losing proposition. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I am in the big blind with 8d 7d, a strong suited connector that performs well in pots. With the pot at 450 and only 200 more to call, I am receiving excellent pot odds to defend my blind. Calling is the standard and most profitable play here to see a flop against Flash's opening range.

The flop comes Ten-Five-Three rainbow. My hand, Eight-Seven suited, has missed this board completely, possessing only back-door straight potential and no flush draw. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flash), checking is the standard and most balanced play to see how he proceeds before committing any more chips to a pot where I currently have very little equity.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I am making a standard continuation bet on this dry flop of Th-5c-3d. This board texture is unlikely to have significantly improved the Big Blind's range, and a half-pot bet allows me to take down the pot immediately while representing a strong hand like a Ten or an overpair.

The flop is T-5-3 rainbow, which misses my 8-7 suited completely. I have no pair and no significant draws, only a weak backdoor straight possibility. My opponent, Flash, is playing aggressively, and calling a continuation bet with 8-high out of position is negative expected value. There is no reason to continue in this hand, so folding is the correct disciplined play to preserve my stack.

T9o is a marginal offsuit connector. Given my position on the button in a 3-handed game, this hand is slightly too weak to open, especially since opponents have shown a willingness to 3-bet light. Folding is the most solid and standard play here to conserve chips.

I am in the Small Blind and the Button has folded. My hand, Queen of Clubs and Eight of Clubs, is a reasonable suited hand that is well-suited to open the pot against the Big Blind. Raising to 300 allows me to exert pressure, attempt to win the blinds immediately, or see a flop with the initiative.

I am in the big blind with 9h 5h. Flashlight, the small blind, has raised to 300. My hand is relatively weak and does not play well out of position against a range that includes many superior hands. Given the current stack sizes and the early stage of the tournament (hand 60 of 150), I prefer to fold and wait for better opportunities rather than defending with a marginal hand.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a strong hand for this position. With the action folded to me, I should open the betting to try and steal the blinds or play in position. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size.

I am in the Small Blind with King of Spades and Five of Spades. Flashlight has raised to 3x the big blind from the Button. While K5s is a suited King and has some playability, it is a relatively weak hand to play out of position against a Button raiser and a strong Big Blind (Pro). Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop without initiative. Given the current tournament stage and stack sizes, I prefer to fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips where I have either the range advantage or position.

AdJd is a premium suited Broadway hand that plays very well as a 3-bet against a Button open. 3-betting out of position applies pressure, builds the pot for value, and often wins the hand uncontested pre-flop.

I opened King-Queen offsuit from the button, and the big blind 3-bet to 1000. Given my position and the strength of KQs, calling is the most balanced approach. Folding would be conceding too much equity, and 4-betting could be overly aggressive against a player who has shown they can play back. I will call the 700 chips to see the flop in position.

As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I maintain a significant range advantage on this Q-high board. Although I completely missed the flop, I have an overcard to the Queen, an Ace-high that has some showdown value, plus backdoor straight and flush draws with the AdJd. A small continuation bet of about 30% of the pot applies pressure to my opponent's floating range and underpairs like 88-JJ, allowing me to take down the pot uncontested or set up a barrel on favorable turn cards.

I have top pair with a King kicker on a relatively dry board. The opponent, who 3-bet pre-flop, has made a small continuation bet of 600 into a 2650 pot. This small sizing suggests they might be cautious or testing the waters with a weaker hand, or potentially an overpair. Calling is the most balanced approach; it keeps the opponent's bluffs in the hand and controls the pot size. Raising would likely isolate me against better hands like AA or KK and force out the weaker hands that I am currently beating. Therefore, calling is the optimal play to proceed to the turn.

The turn 6s doesn't improve my hand, and my backdoor flush draw is now dead. Flashlight called my flop continuation bet, which indicates they likely connected with the Queen, hold a medium pocket pair like 88-JJ, or have a strong club draw. Since a double-barrel is unlikely to fold out their continuing range and I have very little equity, checking and preparing to give up is the most prudent play.

I have top pair with a Queen kicker on a relatively dry board. The opponent's check on the turn after a small continuation bet on the flop suggests a loss of confidence or a weaker holding like a mid-pair or a high-card hand (e.g., AK). Checking back allows the opponent a free card to potentially improve against me, particularly with straight draws like 8-5 or 9-8 now having more equity. Betting around half the pot (1500 into 3250) is the optimal value play; it puts pressure on the opponent's weaker hands and builds the pot while I am likely ahead.

My opponent called my 3-bet pre-flop and my continuation bet on the flop, then fired a substantial bet when checked to on the turn. This indicates they likely have a made hand such as top pair (Qx), a medium pocket pair, or a strong draw. With only Ace-Jack high and no draws to improve, I have very little equity. The most prudent play is to fold and preserve my stack.

I have K8o on the button in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds and attempt to win the pot pre-flop or play in position. My stack is healthy enough to sustain a standard 3x raise.
I am in the Small Blind with 73 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button, folding is the only standard and profitable play.

I am in the big blind with a very weak hand, 2c-Td. Flash has raised to 300 from the button, which is a standard opening range that typically dominates my hand. With no positional advantage and a poor holding, calling the 200 chip deficit is not mathematically profitable. It is better to preserve my stack for a more favorable spot.

Ten-Seven offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop. Even on the button, it is too weak to open-raise as a steal attempt against most opponents, so folding is the optimal and standard play here.

6c 2c is an extremely weak hand with poor equity. Although I am in the small blind and it is a heads-up pot, there is no strategic reason to invest more chips into the pot with such a weak holding. Folding is the most disciplined and prudent play.

I am on the button with Jack-Five offsuit, which is a weak hand with no playability. In a three-handed game, opening with such a weak holding from the button is negative expected value. I will fold and wait for a better spot to enter the pot.

Folded to me in the Small Blind. Ten-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position against a competent player in the Big Blind. While I could attempt a steal or a complete, folding is a disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities where I have a range advantage or position.

I am on the button with K3s, which is a standard opening hand in a 3-handed game. I have been opening for 300 (3bb) consistently in this tournament, and doing so here allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or play in position post-flop.

We are in the Small Blind facing a 3bb button open from Flash. Our hand, 85s, is a bit too loose and speculative to play out of position, even against a wide button range. Folding is the standard and safest play here to preserve our stack for better spots.

I hold a premium hand, Ace-Queen suited, in the big blind. Flash, on the button, has been opening aggressively. A 3-bet is the standard play to isolate the button, take control of the hand, and build a pot while I have the equity advantage. Raising to 900 is a balanced 3-bet size against a 3x opening raise, putting appropriate pressure on the opponent.

I opened from the button with Ks 3s, a standard play in a 3-handed game. Flashlight has 3-bet me from the Big Blind, increasing the price to 900 total. While I have position, K3s is a weak hand to defend against a 3-bet. It is easily dominated by King-high hands (AK, KQ, KJ, KT) and has poor playability on most boards. Although Flashlight has shown a high frequency of 3-betting in previous hands (Hands 26, 32, 51), I prefer to wait for a stronger range to make a stand or 4-bet bluff. My stack is currently the shortest at the table, and I want to avoid getting committed in a bloated pot with a marginal hand.
95s is a very weak 3-gapper and generally not profitable to open from the Button, even in a 3-handed game. Folding is the standard and safest play here to conserve my stack.
I am in the small blind with a weak hand (J2 offsuit) after the button folded. Completing the blind is a standard, low-risk move to see a flop heads-up against the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Six offsuit. Flashlight (Small Blind) has just completed the blind. I have the option to check and see a flop for free or raise. Given that I am the short stack and K6o is a mediocre hand that doesn't play particularly well in a bloated pot out of position, I will take the free flop and check.

I am in the small blind and have hit bottom pair with a Jack kicker on a 2c-8s-7c board. This is a weak, vulnerable hand. Given that I am out of position against the big blind, checking is the most prudent play. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or cause me to fold to a raise, so keeping the pot small is the best strategy.

I have King-high on a dry board after checking my big blind. Since Flashlight checked to me, I will check back to keep the pot small and see a free turn card. My hand has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value or effectively as a bluff here.

The turn Ace of Clubs is a poor card for my bottom pair of deuces. My hand has very little showdown value, especially against a Big Blind's range which frequently contains Aces. Checking is the most prudent decision to keep the pot small and minimize potential losses. If the opponent bets, I will likely fold.

I have King-high on a board with an Ace and three clubs. Flashlight has checked twice, suggesting weakness, but King-high still has some marginal showdown value against a wide small-blind completing range. The pot is very small (200), and there's little benefit to bluffing here when I can take a free river card and potentially improve or win at showdown if the action remains checked down.

The board shows [2c, 8s, 7c, Ac, 6c]. I hold [Js, 2d]. I have a pair of deuces, which is extremely weak and effectively a bluff-catcher with no real showdown value against any made hand. Since the hand has been checked through on all streets, betting would only serve to fold out worse hands (which are rare/non-existent) or run into a better hand. Checking is the only logical play to reach showdown in a small pot.

The board has four clubs and an Ace. I have a pair of sixes with no club. Flashlight has checked every street, indicating extreme weakness or a trap. While my pair of sixes is weak on this texture, the pot is very small (200 chips) and I have some marginal showdown value against hands like low pocket pairs or complete air. There is no merit in betting to value-town myself or turning my pair into a bluff for such a small pot. I will check and take the free showdown.

My hand, King of Diamonds and Four of Spades, is weak and offsuit. It has very low playability in a high-roller environment. Given the requirement to call 100 chips to stay in the hand against the blinds, folding is the correct strategic decision to preserve my stack for more favorable opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket sixes (6c 6s). Since the action folded to me, I am in a blind-vs-blind situation against Pro. Pocket sixes is a very strong hand in this context. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to extract value and potentially take down the pot immediately, or to maintain the initiative going into the flop.
8d 3d is a very weak and disconnected suited hand. Facing a raise from the small blind, the best decision is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 2h 3s, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Although position is an advantage, the hand strength is insufficient to warrant an open raise, especially given that both opponents have larger stacks and have shown a willingness to defend and 3-bet. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Action folded to me in the Small Blind. I have an Ace, which is a great blocker and often the best hand pre-flop against a random Big Blind holding. I will raise to steal the blinds, but I must be cautious if called, given my weak kicker and positional disadvantage.

Holding Jack-Eight offsuit in the Big Blind, I am facing an open raise from the Small Blind. This is a weak hand with poor playability, especially when playing out of position against an opponent's opening range. Given the stack depths and the nature of the hand, there is no compelling reason to defend this holding. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

K5o is a weak hand even on the button. While stealing the blinds is an option in 3-handed play, this hand has very little post-flop playability and poor implied odds. Folding and waiting for a better spot to attack the blinds is the most solid play to preserve our stack.

I hold Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium hand in a heads-up scenario against the big blind. Since the button folded, this is an excellent opportunity to raise and take control of the pot. A raise to 300 is standard and puts appropriate pressure on the opponent.

I have KTo in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Flashlight) raised to 3x. KTo is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I will call to see a flop and play the hand in position, as my hand is likely ahead of a wide SB opening range but not necessarily strong enough to bloat the pot with a 3-bet.

The flop is extremely coordinated with three diamonds, creating a flush possibility and multiple straight draws. While I lack a diamond, I hold two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (requiring a Queen). Given that I raised pre-flop and have the betting initiative, a continuation bet of 300 is appropriate to represent strength and potentially fold out marginal hands that missed this wet board.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a King kicker. The board is monotone (all diamonds), and I do not hold a diamond myself, which is a significant disadvantage. However, Flashlight is betting 300 into a 600 pot (1/3 pot size), giving me 3:1 odds to call. Folding middle pair to a small continuation bet in a blind-vs-blind battle is too conservative. I will call to see the turn, but I must be cautious if another diamond or an overcard appears.

The turn brings the 3 of diamonds, completing a four-flush board. Since I do not hold a diamond, my hand strength has severely diminished. Betting into this board represents a pure bluff that is unlikely to succeed against an opponent who called the flop, as they almost certainly have at least one diamond in their range. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size and evaluate the opponent's intentions, while potentially getting to a cheap showdown if they also check back.

The turn brings a fourth diamond to the board, creating a four-flush. I do not hold a diamond in my hand, meaning any diamond in the opponent's range now beats my pair of tens. Flashlight checked, which could indicate a weak hand without a diamond or a trap with a high diamond. Since I have showdown value with my pair of tens but no diamond to bluff with or protect against, checking behind is the safest play to keep the pot small and see a free river card.

The board has four diamonds, and I do not hold a diamond. With my Ace-high, I have no value to bet and any bet would essentially be a bluff. Given that the turn was checked through, the most rational play is to check behind and take a free showdown, as my opponent is unlikely to fold any diamond or any pair on this wet board.

The board has four diamonds and I do not hold any diamond in my hand. While I have a pair of Tens, any single diamond in my opponent's hand beats me. Flashlight has checked both the turn and the river, suggesting they might not have a diamond or are playing a marginal hand cautiously. Since I have showdown value against hands without a diamond but am unlikely to get called by worse if I bet, checking behind is the safest and most logical play to see the showdown.

I am on the button with Queen-Nine suited, which is a playable hand in late position. Opening the pot with a raise is standard strategy here to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or secure position for the post-flop play.

I have a small pocket pair (33) in the Small Blind. Flashlight raised to 3x from the Button. While 33 is a marginal hand to play out of position, it has high potential if I hit a set. I will call the raise to see a flop and re-evaluate, keeping the pot small for now.

I am dealt 4-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a pre-flop raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, I have a significant range disadvantage and would be out of position post-flop. Calling or raising here has negative expected value, so folding is the only profitable play.

The flop of 9s-Kh-Ac is very unfavorable for my pocket threes, as it contains two overcards that likely hit a pre-flop raiser's range. Being out of position against Flashlight, I will check to see how they respond. If there is any significant betting, I will likely fold, as my hand has very little equity on this board.

The flop texture of Ace-King-Nine is highly unfavorable for a nine-pair, as it hits the calling range of an opponent in the Small Blind very hard. Any Ace or King will easily call a continuation bet, and I gain little value by betting against air. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn while keeping my potential showdown value intact without bloating a pot I am unlikely to win against a calling range that contains many higher pairs.

On a board of Ac-Kh-9s-4h, my pocket threes are very weak. Flashlight raised pre-flop but checked the flop, which could indicate a weak hand or a pot-control line with a marginal made hand like a 9 or a weak Ace. However, since I have almost no equity against any part of their range that calls a bet, and I am out of position, checking is the most prudent play. I will look to fold if a significant bet is made, as I have no draws and very little showdown value.

The board is Ace-King-Nine-Four. My pair of Nines is a marginal hand, essentially functioning as a bluff catcher. The opponent has checked to me twice, which could indicate a weak hand or a check-trap with a strong Ace or King. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands, and it only serves to bloat the pot when I am likely behind. Checking back allows me to maintain pot control and reach a cheap showdown.

The board is extremely coordinated with several overcards (Ace, King, Nine, Eight) to my pocket threes. Flashlight raised pre-flop but has checked both the flop and the turn, which suggests they either have a marginal hand they are taking to showdown or total air. Since my hand has very little showdown value and I am unlikely to fold out a better hand with a single bet on this board, I will check and hope to reach a cheap showdown. If Flashlight bets, I will likely fold as my hand is effectively a bluff-catcher at best.

My opponent has checked down the entire hand, which typically indicates a range comprised of missed draws, low pairs, or perhaps a weak showdown hand. My pair of Nines is a marginal hand that beats many of those missed draws but loses to any Ace, King, or better pair. Betting is unlikely to be called by worse hands, and I gain nothing by turning my hand into a bluff against a range that may have been check-trapping with a strong hand. Checking back is the most profitable play to realize my equity and reach a cheap showdown.

I am on the button with a pocket pair (5h 5d). In a three-handed game, this is a strong opening hand. My standard open throughout this tournament has been 3x the big blind, and I will maintain that consistency here by raising to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand.

I have a premium hand, AKo, in the small blind facing a button open. A 3-bet is mandatory here to extract value, reduce the field, and take the betting initiative out of position. Sizing to around 3.5x to 4x the open (around 1000) is standard.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand, Jack-Deuce offsuit. The action consists of an opening raise from the Button (Flash) followed by a strong 3-bet from the Small Blind (Pro). Given the strength shown by the Small Blind's re-raise and my lack of a playable hand, continuing would be extremely negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined, correct decision.

I am facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind after opening on the button with pocket fives. In a three-handed game, button opening ranges are wide, and consequently, SB 3-betting ranges are also wide. Folding pocket fives here would be too tight given we are playing roughly 75BB deep (based on the initial 100BB start) and I have position. I will call to see a flop, looking to hit a set or navigate a low board using my positional advantage.

I flopped top two pair (Aces and Kings) on a board that features a flush draw. Since AA and KK would typically 4-bet pre-flop, I am almost certainly holding the best hand. I want to start building the pot and extract value from worse Ax hands (like AQ or AJ), KQ, and club flush draws. A continuation bet of about 1/3 of the pot serves to get calls from these weaker hands while setting up an ideal SPR for the later streets.

On this flop of Ace-King-Deuce, my pocket fives have very little equity against the range of a small blind three-bettor. The board heavily favors Pro's range, which likely includes strong Aces, Kings, and broadway hands. Pro is leading out with a continuation bet of 700 into a 2100 pot (making the total pot 2800). While the price is somewhat attractive (4:1), my hand is essentially a bluff-catcher that loses to almost all of Pro's continues. Continuing with pocket fives here would be a waste of chips given the high probability that I am significantly behind and have only two outs to improve. Therefore, I will fold and wait for a better spot.
J4o is a very weak starting hand and falls well outside of any reasonable opening range from the button. Folding is the standard and optimal play.

With the button folding, I am in the small blind heads-up against the big blind. My cards, 8d-Tc, are mediocre but playable in a heads-up situation. Completing the blind is a low-risk way to see a flop and potentially connect with the board, as folding here would be too tight given the positional advantage post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 5h. The Button folded and the Small Blind (Flashlight) completed. With a speculative hand like 9-high suited, I have the option to raise or check. Given my stack size and the fact that I will have position post-flop, checking is the most prudent play to see a free flop and evaluate the hand's potential without unnecessarily inflating the pot.

I am in the small blind with a middle pair (tens) on a very coordinated board of Ace-Queen-Ten. This board is extremely strong for both the big blind and the small blind's calling range, as it connects heavily with high cards. Given that the hand was unraised pre-flop and I only have second pair with a weak kicker, there is no value in betting into a board where I am likely behind any Queen or Ace, which are very common in the big blind's range. Checking is the most standard and defensive play to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

On this flop of Ac-Qc-Th, I have virtually no equity with 9h-5h, aside from a slim backdoor heart draw. The board is highly coordinated and likely hits many hands in Flashlight's completing range from the small blind. Since Flashlight checked to me, the best course of action is to check back, see a free turn card, and avoid inflating the pot with a weak hand.

I have flopped trip tens and improved on the turn with the board showing Queen-Ten-Ace-Ten. This is a very strong hand. With the pot at 200 and no prior betting on this street, I need to bet for value. A bet of 150 puts pressure on my opponent's range, potentially getting called by an Ace or a queen, and charges them to see the river if they are on a straight or flush draw. This is the optimal size to extract value while maintaining a strong position.

On a board of Qc-Th-Ac-Td, my 9h-5h has no showdown value and no remaining draw potential. Flashlight's bet of 150 into the 200 pot on the turn indicates they likely have a piece of this broadway-heavy board or a Ten. Since I have no equity and cannot realistically represent a hand strong enough to bluff effectively on such a coordinated board, folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

Queen-six offsuit is a weak, unplayable hand, even in a three-handed game on the button. There is no strategic value in opening this hand or calling a raise, as it is dominated by most ranges and has poor playability post-flop. Folding preserves my stack size for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with K4s. The Button (Flashlight) has folded. It is just me and the Big Blind (Pro). K4s is a strong enough hand to play heads-up against the Big Blind, especially since I only need to put in 50 more chips to see a flop or I can raise to take the pot now. Given my position and the hand strength, I will raise to 300 to try and win the pot immediately or define the range of the Big Blind.

Facing a small blind open, KTo is a strong enough hand to defend from the big blind. We have position post-flop and calling allows us to keep the pot manageable while realizing our equity against a wide range.

I have bottom pair with a King kicker on an Ace-high board. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, this board favors my perceived range. I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to represent the Ace and attempt to take the pot down immediately. If Pro calls, I have some showdown value with my pair of fours but will need to proceed cautiously on later streets.

I missed the flop completely with King-high and the board features an Ace, which connects well with Flash's pre-flop raising range. Flash has continued with a half-pot bet. I have very little equity and no clear path to win the hand, so folding is the optimal play.

I am on the button with 2c 8s, a very weak hand. In a three-way pot with blinds, it's generally best to fold hands with zero connectivity or high-card value unless there's a specific strategic reason to steal, but 2-8 offsuit is one of the worst hands to play from any position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

98o is a weak offsuit connector. Being out of position in the small blind against the big blind, it is best to simply fold and save chips rather than completing or trying to steal with a marginal holding.

With Jack-Ten offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game, this is a standard open. Raising to 2.5x the big blind (250) applies pressure to the blinds and gives me the initiative going into the flop. JTo has good post-flop playability and blocks some broadway hands.

I am in the small blind holding 5-2 offsuit, which is a very weak hand with almost no equity. Being out of position makes it even harder to play post-flop against an opponent who opened from the button. Folding is the only disciplined choice here to preserve my stack.

I am in the big blind with a pocket pair of fives (5h 5d). Pro has raised to 2.5x from the button. Pocket fives are strong enough to defend here, and given the pot odds (calling 150 to win a total pot of 400), a call is definitely profitable to see a flop. I have a medium stack of 6600, so I want to be cautious but competitive with speculative hands like small pairs that can flop sets.

The flop of 9h-Ts-8d is extremely coordinated and connects very well with the Button's opening range (Pro). My pocket fives are now undercards to the entire board, and any J, Q, 7, or 6 significantly improves many hands. I have very little equity here and no real potential to improve. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor and most likely fold to a bet.

With top pair and an open-ended straight draw on a very connected 9-10-8 board, my hand is very strong but vulnerable to many turn cards. Betting for value and protection is the best play here. A bet of around 350 extracts value from worse pairs, straight draws, and two-pair combinations while charging opponents to see the turn.

I am in the big blind with pocket fives on a very coordinated flop of 9h-Ts-8d. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) on the button, as it connects with many hands in a standard opening range like J7, QJ, T9, and 98. My pocket fives are now an underpair to the board, and I have very little equity to improve. Calling a 63% pot-sized bet here is unprofitable given the high likelihood that I am behind and the number of dangerous turn cards that could further improve my opponent's range or make it impossible for me to continue. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.
Opening from the button with King-Ten offsuit is a standard play to steal the blinds and maintain initiative. A raise of 300 is a balanced size for this stage of the tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. While J9o has some playability, calling from the Small Blind puts me out of position against an aggressive button and leaves me vulnerable to a squeeze from the Big Blind (Pro), who has shown aggression in previous hands. Given the 3x open size and my position, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am dealt Eight-Deuce suited in the Big Blind. Facing a raise from the Button, this hand is far too weak to call or 3-bet, even when suited. Folding is the correct decision here to conserve chips for better spots.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of sixes. This is a strong opening hand in a three-handed game. I will make a standard raise to 300 chips, which is consistent with my previous betting patterns throughout the tournament, to take control of the pot early and potentially win the blinds.
J5 suited is too weak to play from the small blind against a button raise. Folding is the standard and mathematically correct play here.

The starting hand 4s, 3d is extremely weak and provides poor playability post-flop. With AQo in the small blind facing a button open, a 3-bet is standard to extract value, take the betting initiative, and potentially fold out the big blind. A raise to around 1000 puts pressure on the button and defines our hand strength.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (7c 3s) facing an open raise and a 3-bet from the Small Blind. This is a clear fold, as playing a weak hand out of position in a 3-bet pot is highly negative expected value.

I opened with 5h 4h from the button, a standard play with suited connectors to apply pressure. However, Pro has responded with a 3-bet to 1000 from the small blind. While 5h 4h is a good hand for board coverage, calling 700 more (nearly 10% of my stack) is risky against what is likely a strong range from Pro. QTo is a reasonable hand to open from the button in 3-handed play. Raising to 2.5x or 3x applies pressure to the blinds and allows me to take the initiative.

The hand [9s, 2s] is extremely weak and provides poor playability, especially when out of position in the small blind against a button raise. Calling would put me in a disadvantaged position post-flop with a dominated hand. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal strategy to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc 3c and the Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Flashlight in the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 150 more into a pot of 400, giving me very favorable pot odds of nearly 2.7:1. Kc 3c is a decent defending hand in a 3-handed game, as its suitedness and King-high value give it sufficient equity against a wide button opening range. 3-betting is an option, but calling to see a flop is a more stable way to realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position.

I have King-high with a backdoor club flush draw on a Ten-Nine-Deuce board. As the Big Blind, it is standard to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) to see how they respond to this coordinated but relatively low-card board. Donk betting here would likely not accomplish much given the board texture.

I hit top pair with a good kicker on a relatively wet board (Th 9c 2c) that contains straight and flush draws. Betting is essential here to extract value from worse pairs and draws, while also protecting my hand from overcards. A bet of slightly over half pot should accomplish this.

I have flopped a King-high flush draw (Kc, 3c on a board with 9c, 2c). Pro has made a standard continuation bet of 300 into a pot of 550. With a flush draw and an overcard to the board, I have significant equity against Pro's wide button opening range. Calling is the most appropriate action here to realize my equity and see a turn card; it keeps the pot manageable while I am out of position and avoids overplaying the hand before the draw completes.

On the turn, the 4 of hearts is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture or improve my hand. I am still holding a King-high flush draw and one overcard. As the out-of-position player who called the flop bet, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most balanced line. This allows me to see how Pro reacts to the turn card and preserves my stack while I wait to realize my equity on the river.

The 4h on the turn is a blank that doesn't complete any of the obvious flop draws. I still hold top pair with a strong kicker. Flash's check-call on the flop signals a range that includes various draws (clubs, straight draws like J8/QJ) and weaker pairs (9x, worse Tx, medium pocket pairs). Betting the turn is crucial for extracting value from these hands and denying free equity to the numerous draws. A bet of 750 chips (about 65% of the pot) charges draws appropriately to see the river.

I have a King-high flush draw (Kc 3c on a Th 9c 2c 4h board). The pot currently stands at 1900 after Pro's bet of 750, and I need to call 750 to see the river. This gives me pot odds of approximately 28.3%. With 9 outs to a flush (about 19.5% equity), I am not getting direct odds to call. However, considering the implied odds—as Pro has been aggressive and likely has a hand like a strong Ten, an overpair, or even a set that might pay off on the river if the flush completes—calling is justified. Additionally, the King overcard provides a small amount of extra equity if Pro is betting with a hand like middle pair or a straight draw.

On the river, the 8 of clubs completes my King-high flush. The board texture is Ten-Nine-Deuce-Four-Eight with three clubs. Given the action so far, where Pro (the pre-flop aggressor) has bet both the flop and the turn, it is highly likely they hold a strong range consisting of overpairs, sets, or two-pair hands. While the river club is a 'scare card,' these hands are still likely to call a value bet, whereas they might check back if I check to them. Only an Ace-high flush beats me, which is a small portion of Pro's range. Therefore, I will lead out with a value bet of 1500, which is approximately 57% of the pot, to maximize value from Pro's non-flush value hands.

The river brings the 8c, which completes the obvious club flush draw as well as several straight draws (such as J7, QJ, or 76) that could easily be in Flash's flop and turn check-calling range. Flash leading out for 1500 on this highly coordinated board is a very strong signal of a completed draw or a better made hand like two pair. With only a single pair of tens, my hand is reduced to a weak bluff catcher. Given the opponent's line and the scary runout, folding is the only profitable play.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fours. Pocket pairs are strong in this position and merit a raise to isolate the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play heads-up in position. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing to build the pot and extract value if I connect with a set on the flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 3s, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Flashlight has raised from the Button to 300. Even with a decent pot price, 83o has very poor equity and playability out of position. I have the Big Blind yet to act behind me and a raiser in position. This is a standard fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I hold 9d 7d, a suited one-gapper in the Big Blind. Facing a standard 3bb open from the Button and a fold from the Small Blind, I am getting good pot odds to defend. This hand has good playability post-flop, and calling allows me to realize my equity without inflating the pot out of position.

I have a very weak hand on this flop (9-high, backdoor flush draw). As the out-of-position pre-flop caller, the standard play is to check my entire range to the pre-flop aggressor. I will check and evaluate my opponent's action, planning to fold to a bet.

I have flopped bottom set on a board of Js-6d-4h. This is a very strong hand that is currently beating most of Pro's calling range from the big blind. Since Pro checked, I should bet for value to start building the pot and to get paid off by any Jack, lower pair, or straight draw. Betting roughly half the pot (350 into 650) is a balanced sizing that encourages further calls while protecting against potential draws.

On this flop, I only have 9-high with a backdoor flush draw and weak backdoor straight possibilities. Facing a continuation bet from the pre-flop aggressor, I do not have sufficient equity to continue. Folding is the most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

Holding King-Deuce offsuit on the button in a three-handed game is a standard opening situation. Raising to 300 allows me to apply pressure on the blinds and utilize my positional advantage for the rest of the hand. Although the deuce is a weak kicker, the King provides significant high-card value in a short-handed environment.

I have Pocket Aces (Ah As), the absolute best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a standard button open from Flash, I need to 3-bet from the Small Blind to build the pot and extract maximum value. A 3-bet size around 3.3x-3.5x is standard out of position. I will raise to 1000.

Pro's 3-bet from the small blind after Flash's open indicates significant strength, likely a premium range. My hand, King-8 offsuit, is not strong enough to contest a bloated pot, especially while being out of position against two opponents who have shown aggression. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for a more advantageous spot.

I opened from the button with King-Deuce offsuit, which is a standard play to steal the blinds in a three-handed game. However, Pro in the small blind has responded with a 3-bet to 1000. King-Deuce is too weak to continue against a 3-bet, even with positional advantage. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with a weak kicker, and 4-betting is an unnecessary risk at this stage. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

5d 3d is a weak suited one-gapper. Although I have position on the button, this hand lacks the raw equity and playability needed to consistently profit against the blinds. Attempting to steal here with such a marginal holding is unnecessary, so folding is the disciplined and standard play.

With Queen-Seven offsuit in the small blind and the button having folded, I have a heads-up situation against the big blind. It is standard to complete the small blind with this hand to see a flop, as it's a relatively low-cost way to potentially win the pot post-flop given the blinds structure.

I hold King-Jack of Hearts in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has simply completed the blind, which often indicates a wider and potentially weaker range of hands that doesn't want to face a large raise but wants to see a flop. King-Jack suited is a premium hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I will raise to 400 to take control of the pot, extract value from weaker hands in the SB's range, and potentially win the pot immediately.

After limping into the small blind, the big blind raised. Queen-Seven offsuit is a weak hand, and calling a raise out of position against an aggressive opponent is suboptimal. I will fold to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Five offsuit, which is a standard opening hand for this position. Raising to 300 is consistent with the table's betting patterns and allows me to control the pot size and gain initiative against the blinds.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (8c 4h). Flashlight, who is on the button, has raised to 300. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against two opponents, the most professional and mathematically sound decision is to fold and preserve my chips for a more favorable opportunity.
QJo is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind against a Button open. The pot odds are favorable, but I will be playing out of position post-flop so a flat call is the most balanced approach.

As the pre-flop caller playing out of position, it is standard to check the flop to the pre-flop aggressor. I am on the button with Ace-high on a K-Q-2 rainbow board. As the pre-flop aggressor, I should continue with a continuation bet to represent strength, as this board structure hits my opening range well and is likely to cause the opponent to fold if they whiffed. With middle pair (Queens) and a decent Jack kicker, my hand is too strong to fold to a single half-pot continuation bet on this relatively dry King-high board. Flashlight's c-betting range includes many bluffs, gutshots (like JT or AT), and worse pocket pairs. Raising would only isolate me against stronger hands like Kings or better Queens, so flat calling to keep his bluffing range wide and evaluate on the turn is the optimal line.

With middle pair on a K-Q-2-4 board, my hand serves as a solid bluff catcher with decent showdown value, but it's not strong enough to bet for value. The 4 of diamonds turn changes very little, although it introduces a diamond flush draw. Playing out of position, the standard and most balanced play is to check to the pre-flop and flop aggressor. This allows my opponent to either continue bluffing with drawing hands or check behind with marginal value hands, keeping the pot size under control.

I am on the button with Ace-high on a King-Queen-2-4 rainbow board. The opponent called my flop bet, which indicates they likely have a piece of the board, such as a Queen or a King. Betting into this range again on the turn would likely be met with a call, and my Ace-high has very little equity against any pair. Therefore, checking behind is the optimal play to maintain pot control and see a free river card, avoiding further investment with a hand that currently has minimal showdown value.

On the river, the 9 of diamonds completes a potential flush draw and adds to the straight possibilities. With middle pair (Queens) and no diamond in my hand, my showdown value is marginal. A bet would likely only be called by better hands (Kings, flushes, or better Queens) and fold out worse ones. Checking allows me to realize my showdown value or potentially catch a bluff from a missed straight draw.

I have Ace-high with no pair on a board that contains a King, Queen, and a three-flush. The opponent checked the river, and my hand has virtually no showdown value against any pair. Attempting a bluff here is unlikely to succeed, as the opponent called a flop bet and a check-behind on the turn suggests they have some medium-strength holding or a draw that missed. Checking behind is the most prudent play to reach showdown without risking additional chips.

I am holding 8-2 offsuit on the button. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While I have positional advantage, there is no reason to invest chips into a pot with a hand that has such poor equity and playability. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind after my open from the Small Blind. I hold Pocket Twos, the lowest pair in the game. I am on the button with 6d 2s, which is an extremely weak, unsuited, and disconnected starting hand. Even with positional advantage, it's not profitable to play this hand. Folding is the only viable option.

J7 offsuit is a weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand. It plays very poorly post-flop and lacks the equity required to defend from the big blind against a button raise. The best and most standard action here is to fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I will put in a standard opening raise to 300 to take control of the betting and exert pressure on the blinds.

A 3-bet to 950 puts pressure on them and forces them to commit more chips or fold, which fits my strategy of aggressive stack building.

I raised to 300 with Ace-Jack offsuit, a very strong hand in 3-handed play. Flashlight has 3-bet to 950 from the Big Blind. Based on previous hands, Flashlight is quite active with 3-bets. I have flopped top pair with King-Queen on a relatively dry board of 6s-9c-Kd. Given my initiative from the pre-flop 3-bet, I should continue with a continuation bet to extract value from weaker kings, middle pairs, or straight draws. A bet of 650 (approximately one-third of the 1950 pot) is an optimal size to maintain pressure while keeping the opponent invested with a wider range of hands.

Flashlight 3-bet pre-flop and is now following up with a continuation bet on a King-high board. With only Ace-high and no significant draw on this 6-9-K board, I am likely behind and have very little equity to continue. Rather than calling or turning my hand into a high-variance bluff, I will fold to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

AQ suited on the button is a premium starting hand. I will open with a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind to build the pot, take control of the hand, and potentially win the blinds right away or play a heads-up pot in position.

I am in the small blind with Ace-six offsuit. The button has opened, and I am currently the chip leader. A-x offsuit is a decent hand to defend, and rather than just flat calling and playing out of position, I will 3-bet to take the initiative and potentially take down the pot pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Deuce offsuit. The Button opened to 250, and the Small Blind 3-bet to 850. I have Ace-Queen suited on the Button. Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind, AQs is a very strong hand that plays exceptionally well in position. Calling is standard here as it keeps the opponent's weaker broadways and bluffs in their range while allowing me to utilize my positional advantage post-flop. A 4-bet might fold out hands I dominate and isolate me against premium holdings.

I have top pair with an Ace on a relatively dry board. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, a continuation bet is standard to maintain the initiative and gauge the strength of the opponent's hand. Betting roughly 40% of the pot allows me to protect my hand while keeping the pot manageable against the Pro.

With top two pair (Aces and Queens) on a dry rainbow board, my hand is extremely strong and well-disguised. Flashlight's c-bet could be a strong Ace like AK/AJ, pocket pairs like KK/JJ, or pure bluffs. Since I heavily block AA and QQ, the chances of being up against a set are minimal. Calling in position is the optimal play to keep their entire bluffing range and weaker value hands in the pot, while I can extract more value on the turn or river.

The turn King is a dangerous card that significantly hits the calling range of the Pro, likely pairing hands like KQ, KJ, or AK. My top pair with a weak kicker is now significantly devalued and vulnerable to better Aces or two-pair combinations. Checking allows me to pot control and re-evaluate on the river without committing further chips to a pot where I am likely behind.

The turn card is the King of spades, which puts four to a straight and higher two pair combinations on the board. Flashlight's check could indicate pot control with a marginal hand like AJ, giving up a bluff, or a trap with a strong hand like AK, KK, or a straight with JT. Since I have showdown value with Aces and Queens but could easily be crushed by his value range that beats me (AK, KK, JT), betting here is too thin. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, keep his weaker holdings or bluffs in his range, and see a free river where I can still improve to a full house.

The board has paired the Ace on the river, giving me trip Aces with a King-Queen kicker. However, the board is heavily connected (Ace, King, Queen, Seven), and a straight is possible with a J-T combination. The turn was checked by both players, which suggests the Pro likely does not have a monster full house, but they could still be holding a straight. Given the risks, checking is the most prudent action to control the pot, avoid running into a better hand, and potentially induce a bluff.

On the river, the board pairs the Ace, giving me Aces full of Queens. This is an incredibly strong hand that only loses to AK (Aces full of Kings) and the unlikely JsTs Royal Flush. My opponent's check-check line on the turn and river suggests he has marginal showdown value, such as Trip Aces with AJ/AT, a lesser full house like KK, or perhaps a straight with JT. Since I crush this range, a value bet is mandatory. Betting half-pot targets these exact hands, giving them a reasonable price to call while extracting maximum value.

The board is heavily connected with Ace-King-Queen-Seven-Ace. I have trip Aces with a 6-kicker (the worst possible trip Aces), which is effectively just a bluff catcher at this point. The Pro checked the turn, which could indicate a missed draw or a slow-played hand. However, checking the turn makes it less likely they have a monster hand, though the river Ace is a very scary card that could have completed a straight, flush, or boat. Given the pot odds of 4:1 and the fact that I am a Pro player, calling to induce a bluff or catch a split pot if the Pro has the final Ace is a viable strategy, despite the likelihood of being beat by a better Ace or a stronger made hand.
I am on the button with King-Eight suited. In a three-handed game, this hand has sufficient equity to open the pot. A standard raise allows me to take the initiative and potentially steal the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Nine-Deuce of diamonds. While suited, this hand is extremely weak and ranks near the bottom of my range. The button (Flashlight) has opened for a 3x raise to 300. I am out of position against both the original raiser and a deep-stacked, aggressive Big Blind. Calling or raising here with such a marginal hand is a losing play in the long run. Pocket Jacks is a premium hand, significantly ahead of a standard Button opening range. Given we are out of position in the Big Blind, a 3-bet is necessary to extract value and take initiative, while also denying cheap equity. Raising to 1000 is a standard 3-bet size here.

The big blind has 3-bet my initial opening raise from the button. With suited King-Eight, I have decent post-flop playability and sufficient pot odds (roughly 2:1) to call. Given the opponent's aggressive 3-betting history, I will call the additional 700 to see a flop and re-evaluate.

The Ace on the flop is a terrible card for our pocket Jacks, as it hits a large portion of our opponent's 3-bet calling range (like AK, AQ, AJ, AT). Being out of position, checking for pot control and taking a defensive line is the most sensible play. Betting would only get called or raised by better hands (Ax, sets) while folding out hands we already beat.

The flop is Ace-high, which hits the opponent's 3-bet range very hard. Pro checking suggests they either have the Ace and are trapping, or they missed and are checking to re-evaluate. With King-high, I have minimal showdown value and no strong draw, so betting serves little purpose as it would only be called by better hands. Checking behind is the most disciplined play to control the pot size and see a free card on the turn, preserving my stack.

The turn card is a blank. Since our opponent checked back the flop, their range likely consists of marginal made hands like medium pocket pairs (88-QQ), missed broadway cards, or a weak Ace that's pot controlling. Betting now will likely only get called by better hands (like an Ace or QQ) and fold out the hands we are currently beating. Checking again keeps the pot manageable, controls risk, and might induce a bluff from missed overcards on the river.

Pro has checked the turn after checking the flop, which indicates they may be weak or holding a medium pocket pair that they are wary of playing on this Ace-high board. However, I hold King-high with no pair and only a backdoor draw. Betting now would be an unnecessary bluff against a player who 3-bet pre-flop and likely won't fold an Ace or even a pair. Checking behind is the most disciplined option to maintain pot control and realize my equity or get to a cheap showdown.

The river brings a third heart and pairs the six. A value bet here is extremely thin, as better hands (like any Ace, a rivered flush, or a larger pocket pair) will call or raise, while worse hands will almost certainly fold. Checking allows me to realize my decent showdown value with pocket Jacks cheaply, while potentially inducing a bluff from a missed draw.

The hand has reached the river and my opponent has checked. My King-high has no showdown value against the opponent's 3-bet range, which is heavily weighted towards high cards and pocket pairs. While the opponent checking three times suggests they might have a weak hand or a medium pair, bluffing into a range that contains many Aces or small pairs is unlikely to succeed. Checking behind is the optimal play to reach the showdown without committing more chips to a pot I am unlikely to win.

On the button in a 3-handed game, Jack-Nine offsuit is a standard opening hand. I'll raise to 300 to exert pressure and try to take down the blinds or play a pot in position. I've noticed both opponents have been active with 3-bets recently, so I will be prepared to fold if the action gets too heavy behind me.
With 64o in the small blind facing a raise from the button, my hand is far too weak to call or 3-bet. Folding is the standard and correct decision.

I am in the Big Blind with a weak hand (King-Four offsuit). Most Kings in the opponent's range dominate mine. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for a stronger situation.

9c 2s is a pure trash hand. Even from the Button, it is completely unplayable. With a comfortable chip lead, there's no need to play marginal or junk hands. I will simply fold and wait for a better spot.

Pocket eights is a strong hand that should be played aggressively. With the button having folded and being in the small blind against the big blind (Flash), I want to take control of the hand pre-flop. I am in the Big Blind with 7-4 offsuit. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Small Blind after the Button folded. With King-Ten offsuit on the button, I have a strong enough hand to open the betting and leverage my position against the blinds. An open raise to 300 is standard to build the pot and potentially win the blinds or play the hand with the initiative.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Deuce offsuit. The button (Flashlight) has opened for a 3x raise to 300. Jack-Deuce is a very weak hand with poor playability and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Facing a 3x open from the Button while in the Big Blind, 87o is too weak and disconnected to defend profitably. Being out of position makes realizing equity difficult. A fold is the standard and most mathematically sound play here.

I am on the button with Ten-Trey suited. While being on the button allows for a wide opening range in a three-handed game, this hand is at the bottom of that range. Flashlight (Big Blind) has been aggressive with 3-bets recently, and Pro (Small Blind) is also playing quite actively. There is no reason to invest any chips with such a weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand, even in a blind versus blind situation. Folding is the optimal and standard play.

With a weak hand like 6d-8s in the small blind and only 50 more chips required to call to see the flop against the big blind, I will complete the bet. This allows me to see a cheap flop in position relative to the big blind.

I have complete air on an Ace-high board, holding only eight-high. There is no strategic value in betting here as I have no equity against any pair or Ace, and my opponent in the big blind is likely to have hit the Ace or at least have a pair. I will check, planning to fold to any significant bet.

On this board of 3c-3h-As, I have Ten high. Flashlight limped from the Small Blind and checked the flop. The board shows two Aces and two Treys, effectively neutralizing high-card hands. My 8-high hand has zero equity here. Betting would be a pure bluff into a board that strongly favors any Ace or better. Given the current pot of 200 and the fact that my opponent likely has at least a pocket pair or an Ace, checking is the most prudent and strategic play to minimize losses.

The board is now double-paired with Aces and Threes (As-Ac-3c-3h). I hold Ten-high. In this double-paired board situation, my hand is currently two pair, Aces and Threes, with a Ten kicker. There is no reason to bet into a tiny pot with no real equity or fold equity against a random range. I will check to see the river for free.

The board is paired with Aces and Treys, and the action has been checked down through all three streets. My hand (8-high) has no showdown value and essentially no fold equity, as any hand that checked through likely has some marginal showdown value or is also a complete air-ball. Betting here would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to succeed against any portion of the opponent's checking range. Therefore, checking to go to showdown is the only logical play.

The board is double-paired with Aces and Threes, and a Jack is also on the board. My hole cards are a Ten and a Four, both of which are lower than the Jack. There is no value in betting here, as any hand that calls me likely has me beaten or is also playing the board. I will check and take the showdown.

We are on the Button with 10-6 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. With two players still to act in the blinds and no significant structural pressure to steal with any two cards, the optimal and standard play is to fold.

I hold a pocket pair of fours in the small blind with only 50 chips required to call the big blind. Given the pot odds and my position, calling to see a flop is a standard, low-risk way to realize the equity of my pocket pair.

I hold Jack-Five offsuit, which is a weak hand in a three-handed game. Regardless of my position as the button, this hand lacks the strength to contest the pot against two other players. Folding is the most prudent strategy to preserve my stack for a more favorable hand.

Facing a 3bb open from the Small Blind, holding 97 offsuit. This hand is too weak and disconnected to defend, even with position. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

With Ace-high on a paired, dry board (2h Kh Ks), it's highly likely I have the best hand or can easily fold out the equity of my opponent's marginal hands. I have flopped trips on a very dry board (K-K-2). My opponent, Pro, has made a standard continuation bet after raising pre-flop. At this stage, raising would likely force out most of Pro's bluffs or weaker hands like pocket pairs that might still be drawing thin. By calling, I keep Pro's entire range in the pot and allow him to continue bluffing or potentially value-betting a hand like Ace-high or a medium pocket pair on the turn. There are very few cards that can hurt me, so slow-playing here maximizes the potential value I can extract if Pro continues his aggression.

On the turn, the board has become significantly more coordinated with the Ten of Hearts, which completes some potential flushes and straights (like QJ). However, I still have three of a kind with Kings. While my kicker is weak, leading out here (a donk bet) would likely fold out most of Pro's bluffs and only get called by hands that either beat me or have significant equity. By checking, I allow Pro to continue with his aggression if he's bluffing or value betting a worse hand like a pocket pair. It also serves as a pot-control measure in case the heart on the turn gave him a flush or a better King.

The turn brings the Ten of Hearts, completing the obvious flush draw while the board is paired with Kings. I hold Ace-high with the Four of Hearts, giving me a very weak flush draw. Betting here is risky because my opponent's flop calling range includes Kings, flush draws that just got there, or pocket pairs that might not fold. Checking behind is the safest play to keep the pot small, realize whatever marginal equity I have, and see a free river card.

I have three of a kind (Kings) with a 6 kicker. The board is 2h Kh Ks Th 7s, which includes three hearts. Pro raised pre-flop and continuation-bet the flop, but checked the turn. This check on the turn often signals either a marginal hand (like a medium pocket pair or a weak Ten) or a missed draw. However, it could also be a trap or a pot-control move with a flush or a better King. By checking the river, I allow Pro to bluff with any missed draws (like QJ or missed hearts) that he would otherwise fold to a lead. Additionally, if Pro has a better King or a flush, checking prevents me from losing additional chips to a raise. Given the board texture and action, check-calling is the most profitable line to extract value from bluffs while controlling the size of the pot.

The river brings a brick with the Seven of Spades. Flash has checked to me again. I have Ace-high, which actually holds some decent showdown value against missed draws or weaker Ace-highs that floated the flop. Trying to bluff here is risky because I will only get called by better hands like small pocket pairs, Tens, or slow-played Kings/flushes. Checking behind allows me to get to a free showdown, realize my equity, and avoid losing additional chips when I'm beat.

On the Button with Queen-Jack offsuit, which is a strong starting hand 3-handed. I will open with a standard raise to pressure the blinds.

I have Queen-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind. On this flop of 3-9-4, I have two overcards to the board. Flashlight is leading out with a half-pot bet of 300. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have lost the initiative on this wet board, and checking is the most strategic play to control the pot size and re-evaluate my opponent's reaction.

On this turn, the five of spades completes a possible straight and brings a third spade to the board. I have the ten of spades, giving me a flush draw. Flashlight, who raised pre-flop and bet the flop, has now checked. This check often indicates either a marginal hand that wants to see a cheap showdown or a complete miss. While I could bet to try and take the pot now, checking behind allows me to realize my equity for free. If a spade hits the river, I will have a strong hand, and if a Queen or Ten hits, I might also take the lead. Checking is the lowest-risk play that keeps the pot manageable while I'm still in a drawing phase.

Since I cannot win at showdown and a bluff-raise is too risky given the board texture and my previous check on the turn, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for the remaining hands.
